title: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
steps: Check the scene for safety and the person for responsiveness.
Call emergency services immediately if no response.
Place the person on their back on a firm, flat surface.
Give 30 chest compressions (2 inches deep, 100-120 bpm).
Give 2 rescue breaths if trained, or continue compressions only.
Repeat until professional help arrives or an AED is available.

title: Severe Hemorrhage
steps: Apply direct pressure to the wound with a clean cloth or gauze.
Maintain pressure without lifting the cloth to check the wound.
If the dressing becomes soaked, add move gauze on top (do not remove original).
Elevate the injury above the heart level if possible.
If bleeding is life-threatening and on a limb, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
Monitor for signs of shock (pale skin, rapid pulse, shallow breathing).

title: Choking (Victim / Alone)
steps: Try to cough as hard as you can.
If you cannot breathe or cough, perform self-Heimlich: Lean over a firm object (back of a chair, edge of a table or counter).
Press your upper abdomen hard against the edge to force the object out.
Repeat until the object is expelled.
Call 112/911 or signal for help immediately after clearing the airway.

title: Choking on Food or Object (Witness / Response)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' If they can speak or cough, do not intervene.
If they cannot speak or breathe, stand behind them and perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich).
Alternate with 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades.
Continue until the object is expelled or the person loses consciousness.
If they lose consciousness, start CPR immediately and call 112/911.

title: Severe Burns
steps: Stop the burning process: Remove from heat or douse with water.
Run cool (not cold) water over the burn for at least 10-20 minutes.
Remove jewelry or tight clothing before swelling begins.
Cover the burn loosely with a sterile, non-stick bandage or plastic wrap.
Do NOT apply ice, butter, or ointments to a severe burn.
Do NOT pop blisters.

title: Stroke (FAST)
steps: FACE: Ask the person to smile. Does one side of the face droop?
ARMS: Ask the person to raise both arms. Does one arm drift downward?
SPEECH: Ask the person to repeat a simple phrase. Is their speech slurred or strange?
TIME: If you observe any of these signs, call emergency services immediately.
Note the time when symptoms first appeared.
Keep the person calm and lying down until help arrives.

title: Seizure Response
steps: Gently place the person on the floor and clear the area of hard or sharp objects.
Place something soft (like a folded jacket) under their head.
Turn the person onto their side to keep the airway clear.
Do NOT put anything in their mouth (risk of injury to you or them).
Do NOT restrain the person; let the seizure run its course.
Time the seizure; call emergency services if it lasts more than 5 minutes.

title: Poisoning
steps: Try to identify the substance and the amount ingested.
Call the local Poison Control Center or emergency services immediately.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a professional.
If the substance is on the skin or eyes, flush with water for 15-20 minutes.
If the person is unconscious but breathing, place them on their side.
Save the container or label to show to medical personnel.

title: Drowning / Submersion / Water Rescue
steps: Get the person out of the water as quickly as possible without endangering yourself.
Check for responsiveness and breathing.
If not breathing, start rescue breathing immediately (even in the water if safe).
Perform CPR (30 compressions, 2 breaths) as soon as on a firm surface.
If person vomits, turn their head to the side to prevent choking.
Always seek medical attention, even if the person seems fine (risk of secondary drowning).

title: Anaphylaxis (Victim / Self-Care)
keywords: I am having an allergic reaction
steps: Use your epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately. Inject into outer thigh and hold for 3-10 seconds.
Call 112/911 or signal for help. State: 'Anaphylaxis'.
Lie flat on your back with your feet elevated.
Unlock your front door if you are alone so paramedics can enter.
Note the time of injection and keep the device for the medical team.

title: Anaphylaxis (Witness / Responder)
steps: Call 112/911 immediately.
Ask if they have an EpiPen and help them find it.
Administer the auto-injector if they cannot: Press firmly into their outer thigh (can be through clothing).
Have the person lie flat with feet elevated. If they are vomiting, turn them on their side.
Monitor breathing. Be prepared to start CPR if they stop breathing.

title: Hypothermia / Cold Exposure
steps: Move the person out of the cold and into a dry, warm location.
Gently remove wet clothing and replace with dry blankets or clothes.
Warm the person gradually; start with the center of the body (chest, neck, groin).
Provide warm, non-alcoholic, non-caffeinated beverages if they are conscious.
Do NOT use direct heat (hot water, heating pads) or rub limbs, as this can cause heart rhythm issues.
Keep the person lying down and monitor breathing until medical help arrives.

title: Fractures and Dislocations
steps: Do NOT attempt to move the person unless there is an immediate danger.
Do NOT try to realign the bone or push it back in.
Apply a sterile dressing to any open wounds before splinting.
Immobilize the injured area: Use a split (folded cardboard, wood) that extends beyond the joints above and below the break.
Apply ice packs to reduce swelling (do not apply directly to skin).
Check for circulation (pulse/warmth) below the injury site after splinting.

title: Diabetic Emergency
steps: If the person is conscious and can swallow, give them 15-20g of fast-acting sugar (fruit juice, soda, candy).
Wait 15 minutes and repeat if they do not feel better.
If they are unconscious: Call emergency services immediately. Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
If they are carrying a Glucagon kit, it may be administered by a trained person.
Note the time and what was given to inform the paramedics.

title: Syncope (Fainting)
steps: Lay the person flat on their back and elevate their legs about 12 inches (if no injury).
Loosen any tight clothing (collars, belts) to improve circulation.
Check for breathing and responsiveness; if they don't wake up quickly, call emergency services.
If they vomited, turn them onto their side to prevent choking.
Do NOT splash water on their face or try to give them anything to drink until fully conscious.
Keep them lying down for at least 10-15 minutes after they wake up.

title: Panic Attack (Victim / Self-Grounding)
keywords: I am having a panic attack
steps: Focus on your breathing: Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 4, exhale for 4 (Box Breathing).
Ground yourself (5-4-3-2-1): Identify 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
Remind yourself: 'This is a panic attack. It is temporary. I am safe.'
Splash cold water on your face or hold an ice cube to trigger the 'dive reflex' and slow your heart rate.
Call a trusted contact or a crisis line if you need someone to talk you through it.

title: Panic Attack (Witness / Co-Regulation)
keywords: she is having a panic attack
how to help someone with anxiety
steps: Stay calm and speak in a soft, steady voice. Do not tell them to 'just relax'.
Guide them through slow breathing: 'Breathe with me, slowly in... and out.'
Move them to a quiet, less crowded area if possible.
Ask: 'What do you need right now?' or 'Can I hold your hand?'
Stay with them until the attack subsides. Do not leave them alone.

title: Opioid Overdose
steps: Check for responsiveness and signs of breathing.
If the person is not breathing or struggling to breathe, call emergency services immediately.
If available, administer Naloxone (Narcan) following the package instructions.
Perform CPR if the person is not breathing and has no pulse.
Stay with the person until emergency help arrives; Naloxone may wear off, and the person could relapse into overdose.
Do NOT leave the person alone or try to make them vomit.

title: Heart Attack (Victim / Alone)
keywords: I think I'm having a heart attack
steps: Stop all physical activity immediately. Sit or lie down.
Call 112/911. State: 'I am alone and having a heart attack.'
If you are not allergic, chew and swallow one adult aspirin (325mg) or four baby aspirins.
Unlock your front door so emergency responders can get in.
Stay as calm as possible and keep the phone line open.

title: Heart Attack (Witness / Responder)
keywords: he is having a heart attack
steps: Call 112/911 immediately.
Have the person sit down and rest. Loosen tight clothing.
Ask if they take nitroglycerin or other heart medication and help them take it.
If they are conscious and not allergic, give them aspirin to chew.
Prepare to perform CPR if they lose consciousness and stop breathing.

title: Heat Stroke (Hyperthermia)
steps: Move the person to a cool, shaded area immediately.
Call emergency services; heat stroke is a life-threatening medical emergency.
Cool the person rapidly by spraying with cool water, fanning, or placing ice packs/cool wet cloths on the neck, armpits, and groin.
Do NOT give the person anything to drink if they are confused or vomiting.
Monitor their temperature and continue cooling efforts until help arrives.
If they lose consciousness, check breathing and pulse.

title: Electric Shock (Electrocution)
steps: Do NOT touch the person if they are still in contact with the electrical source.
If possible, turn off the source of electricity; if not, use a non-conducting object (wood, plastic) to move the source away.
Call emergency services immediately.
Once the person is safe from the current, check for breathing and pulse; begin CPR if necessary.
Treat any visible burns with cool water and sterile dressings.
Keep the person lying down and warm to prevent shock until help arrives.

title: Penetrating Trauma (Stab / Gunshot)
steps: Ensure the scene is safe before approaching the victim.
Call emergency services immediately.
Apply direct pressure to the wound to control bleeding, UNLESS there is an object embedded (do NOT remove it).
If the wound is in the chest and making a 'sucking' sound, seal it with an airtight material (plastic wrap, foil) on three sides only.
Keep the person still and warm; monitor for signs of shock.
Do NOT give the person anything to eat or drink.

title: Childbirth / Labor
steps: Call emergency services immediately.
Help the person into a comfortable position, usually lying on their back with knees bent and apart.
Place clean towels or cloths under them.
Stay calm and encourage slow, deep breaths.
Do NOT try to delay the birth or push the baby back.
Once the baby is born, wrap them in a clean, warm towel and place them on the mother's chest.

title: Neurological Emergencies / Motor Block
steps: Ask the person if they have a known neurological condition (e.g., Parkinson's).
If they are 'frozen' or cannot move, do NOT pull or force them to walk.
Stay calm and encourage them to take a deep breath.
Try 'cueing': Ask them to step over an imaginary line or your foot to break the freeze.
Ensure they are in a safe position where they won't fall.
If the condition is sudden or accompanied by confusion, call emergency services.
